Iranian media accused Barak Ravid of spreading false reports after he claimed Iran and the U.S. were nearing a deal.

The report briefly pushed oil prices sharply lower, reversing gains tied to the conflict, before prices recovered following denials from Iranian officials.

The article questioned how Western journalists gain access to sensitive White House information and suggested the reports were aimed at influencing global markets.

text: Next weeks Menu: Jerome Powell last day as FED chair is Friday May 15th Macro: - Tues: CPI - Wed: PPI 3 narratives/baskets of trades right now: 1) Oil (WTI/Brent): News Trading the headlines & news feed. All sides are manipulating and lying, requires lots of screen time & patience & keeping up. This has been the trade I focused the most on past few weeks, I dont expect anything to happen in the short-term regarding Hormuz (it stays closed) 2) AI (Nasdaq/Single Name Stocks): Capex & Compute Demand trade. Some of the craziest ever returns in the stock market, bet on AI/semis & everything related 3) Crypto (BTC): Benefiting from 2) doing record numbers as Beta. Overall positive sentiment returning to crypto. Stocks up -> BTC up. Other positive factors for crypto: -STRC should re-peg this week -> Saylor buying a lot & BTC spot ETFs been frontrunning it a bunch (market got more sophisticated regarding STRC) - Crypto legislation/regulation (Senate Banking Committee / Clarity bill etc) got deadlines this week (May 14th) so a decent chance for some sort of headline? - Stablecoin MCAP is back to growing My posts from past few weeks make it obvious, which markets im personally focusing on. What will be really interesting to me is, how will market trade around new FED chair (maybe absolutely nothing happens lol) as for the 3) categories of trades, i consider them fairly independent of each other edited 2.6K  07:25 ✦ ✦ 
raw_text:
Next weeks Menu:

Jerome Powell last day as FED chair is Friday May 15th

Macro:
- Tues: CPI
- Wed: PPI

3 narratives/baskets of trades right now:
1) Oil (WTI/Brent): News Trading the headlines & news feed. All sides are manipulating and lying, requires lots of screen time & patience & keeping up. This has been the trade I focused the most on past few weeks, I dont expect anything to happen in the short-term regarding Hormuz (it stays closed)

2) AI (Nasdaq/Single Name Stocks): Capex & Compute Demand trade. Some of the craziest ever returns in the stock market, bet on AI/semis & everything related

3) Crypto (BTC): Benefiting from 2) doing record numbers as Beta. Overall positive sentiment returning to crypto.  Stocks up -> BTC up.

Other positive factors for crypto:
-STRC should re-peg this week -> Saylor buying a lot & BTC spot ETFs been frontrunning it a bunch (market got more sophisticated regarding STRC)
- Crypto legislation/regulation (Senate Banking Committee / Clarity bill etc) got deadlines this week (May 14th) so a decent chance for some sort of headline?
- Stablecoin MCAP is back to growing

My posts from past few weeks make it obvious, which markets im personally focusing on.

What will be really interesting to me is, how will market trade around new FED chair (maybe absolutely nothing happens lol)

as for the 3) categories of trades, i consider them fairly independent of each other
edited
2.6K

07:25

text: Forwarded from Walter Bloomberg HORMUZ CLOSURE COULD REMOVE 100M BARRELS WEEKLY, SAYS ARAMCO CEO Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned that a continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz could cut global oil supply by about 100 million barrels per week. He said the market has already lost around 1 billion barrels due to the disruption, partly offset by alternative routes and reserves. Nasser called it the biggest energy supply shock ever, noting the crisis could delay full market normalization until 2027 if it drags on. Aramco is expanding non-Hormuz export routes, including its Red Sea terminal, to improve resilience as the conflict continues to disrupt global flows. (@WalterBloomberg) 2K  21:48
raw_text:
Forwarded from

Walter Bloomberg
HORMUZ CLOSURE COULD REMOVE 100M BARRELS WEEKLY, SAYS ARAMCO CEO

Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned that a continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz could cut global oil supply by about 100 million barrels per week.

He said the market has already lost around 1 billion barrels due to the disruption, partly offset by alternative routes and reserves.

Nasser called it the biggest energy supply shock ever, noting the crisis could delay full market normalization until 2027 if it drags on.

Aramco is expanding non-Hormuz export routes, including its Red Sea terminal, to improve resilience as the conflict continues to disrupt global flows.
(@WalterBloomberg)
2K

